data.title: Acurx Ready To Expand Pipeline With Anthrax Treatment
data.description:
Readers familiar with my prior research on Acurx Pharmaceuticals (NASDAQ:ACXP) know that I have a generally negative view on the antibiotics subsector of drug development due to many new antibiotics being used as a last resort. In this report, I will show why I have maintained my favorable opinion of Acurx since my last analysis. I believe that the company is undervalued and that its antibiotics have market potential, which is rare in the antibiotics investment landscape.

About Acurx Pharmaceuticals

For those unfamiliar with Acurx, the company is a small biotech company developing a new class of antibiotics. They are focusing on infections caused by Gram-positive bacteria listed as priority pathogens by the WHO, CDC, and FDA, and are in the process of planning and starting international phase 3 trials for their lead asset, ibezapolstat, an inhibitor of bacterial DNA polymerase IIIC (pol IIIC) for the treatment of C. difficile infection. The company’s phase 2 clinical trial testing ibezapolstat treatment of C. diff infection (CDI) in the second half of 2023 surprised investors which more than tripled the stock in a short period of time, but the company’s balance sheet appears to be the more immediate concern. Since there haven’t been any new FDA-approved antibiotic classes in decades, FDA approval of ibezapolstat could be a big deal and its Phase 2b data demonstrates clinical comparability to standard of care oral vancomycin, while preserving the microbiome and avoiding reinfections. The company is targeting first-line therapy for CDI, which could be a billion-dollar opportunity. Additionally, Merck’s (MRK) fidaxomicin, also a co-standard of care, went off patent earlier this year, so there could be strategic interest.

Update on Lead Program: Ibezapolstat

Ibezapolstat demonstrated perfect results in the initial readout of its phase 2a trial, with all patients demonstrating a cure from C. diff infection (10 of 10). The follow-up data on this trial in phase 2b was good but was not perfect. Overall, clinical trial results indicate promising outcomes, with ibezapolstat demonstrating a higher clinical cure rate and lower recurrence rates in patients with C. diff infections compared to standard therapies. Overall, ibezapolstat cured 25 of 26 patients at end of treatment for a 96% cure rate in Phase 2. These findings position ibezapolstat as a potential game-changer in the treatment landscape for this challenging infection, as C. diff exhibits no preexisting resistance to the drug. This makes Acurx an attractive candidate for potential M&A. Notably, the drug does not have a deleterious effect on the gut microbiome compared to vancomycin and fidaxomicin, which I reviewed in my prior articles, the initial analysis, and a first follow-up.

Acurx is planning international phase 3 trials for ibezapolstat to pursue global approvals. The company is prepping a filing package for the EMA, and they expect 125 trial sites, with at least 60 of those sites in Europe and some in Canada and the US. The FDA-agreed-on design will be similar to the phase 2 trial, 1:1 randomization against vancomycin, with 450 patients enrolled in each of two phase 3 trials, testing for non-inferiority and then superiority of CDI clinical cure and reduction of CDI recurrence. The cost is expected to be $25m for each trial, which can be done concurrently or sequentially. With a total cost of $50 million required to complete this endeavor, Acurx will need to raise money to complete these trials, but that will be reviewed later in this article. The phase 3 manufactured product is on track to be ready in early November, and many of the trial sites will be ready to enroll by the end of Q1 2025. Unless there are persisting financial problems, enrollment will start by the end of Q1.

The company’s scientific team found a way to tell whether CDI patients are going to reinfect when given any therapy to treat CDI, based on a microbiome signature. The company will continue to monitor this proactive approach in Phase 3 and will consider building a test kit for treating physicians so that they can tell days into treatment whether the patients have a high probability of reinfection, which will help drive ibezapolstat sales as the company was recently granted a patent on treating CDI while preserving and restoring the microbiome which has the effect of reducing recurrence. Physicians could switch patients over to ibezapolstat if they are advised by the test that a patient will be reinfected. Given the recurrent CDI (rCDI) market is a $4.7 billion cost to the US public health system, the market opportunity for these test kits could be quite substantial.

Acurx also unveiled interesting research that explained ibezapolstat’s lack of cross-resistance with other antibiotics, information that can be used to foster the development of further compounds.

Update on ACX-375

Acurx had work done at the University of Florida to study MIC (minimum inhibitory concentration) for anthrax, a Bioterrorism Category A pathogen that, like C. diff, also requires the pol 3c enzyme to build new bacteria from a host cell by making DNA for cell replication. The company showed particularly good activity against ciprofloxacin-resistant anthrax. Cipro is a first-line treatment stockpiled by the government for anthrax but is a 40-year-old antibiotic, and anthrax appears to be developing a resistance to ciprofloxacin, which is a first-line treatment for bioterrorism anthrax. There also doesn’t appear to be much else going on in anthrax antibiotic research, so this could be a high-priority program with which Health and Human Services ('HHS') may have a significant interest.

The interesting thing about anthrax is that it's not possible to run clinical trials for this indication. So, the way to get FDA approval is to get a different indication approved, and then the FDA will extend the label for anthrax (and perhaps stockpile) based on the results of animal studies. The company’s path will likely be to study ACX-375 in MRSA infections and then get the drug’s label extended for anthrax. That sounds like extra money to be spent, but the company has the potential to receive some significant aid for the development of this program, including the MRSA drug development through a partnership with HHS.

Financing: the Crux of the Issue

As is common with pre-revenue biotech companies like Acurx, financing drug development through clinical trials is a primary concern. However, it can be particularly problematic when a company has a market cap that does not support raising funds for the next stage of development. This is the case with Acurx, but fortunately, the company has several other avenues to raise money to move forward without too much shareholder dilution. I believe that fundraising activities will be the greatest near-term catalyst for the stock as they are, in my opinion, the current issue holding shares back from making new highs.

Fundraising Opportunities

With a market cap of $33 million, Acurx cannot raise $50 million or more to pursue 2 phase 3 trials. However, with other opportunities to raise money coming through, the company may be able to supplement with traditional equity fundraising if necessary.

The company has a meeting with BARDA on October 29th, 2024. BARDA already has a scope for an open basket of items, including anthrax, and they may decide to fund the C. diff program, too. Management has already met with both sides of the political aisle in Washington, DC to enlist support for the company’s activities to combat antimicrobial resistance in the major markets of C difficile, MRSA and Anthrax. We believe BARDA funding of one or more of these three programs, given the state of the worldwide fight against antimicrobial resistance, is quite likely. In September 2024, the UN general assembly met in New York and at the meeting the WHO, put out a “call to arms” for each member state to come back to the WHO with an action plan for how each member state will combat antimicrobial resistance including a plan for how to finance and foster development of new chemical classes of antibiotics to avoid antimicrobial resistant infections from becoming the top cause of death worldwide. So the company has quite reasonable non-dilutive government partnership possibilities with BARDA, NIH, Carb-X and under the Pasteur Act which may be approved in the upcoming months.

Next in line is Project BioShield, which in management’s view has a higher probability of pulling through for Acurx. Signed into law in 2004, this allows the government to accelerate the development of medical countermeasures (MCMs) against chemical, biological, radiological, and nuclear (CBRN) threats to our national security. Acurx is asking HHS, through the bioindustry group and antimicrobial group (AWG), to increase or modify the scope of material threat determination to include therapies that treat antimicrobial-resistant pathogens, so that QIDP antibiotics would qualify for approval under Project BioShield and potentially receive funding for DOD stockpiling. If Acurx’s C. diff antibiotic candidate qualifies as a “material threat designation,” Project BioShield would pay for the entire ibezapolstat phase 3, and the government would subsequently stockpile the drug, similarly to how the PASTEUR Act (not signed yet) includes a stockpile for Medicare, Medicaid, and the VA.

Speaking of the PASTEUR Act—PASTEUR legislation might have to have a dichotomy—a drafting to limit the benefit of PASTEUR for larger pharma companies vs incubator companies, including some sort of phase-out, as to balance the risks and benefits for smaller vs larger companies. Although this legislation is still in Congress in draft form, the company believes it has a high likelihood of being signed into law by the end of 2024.

Lastly, the company is still working on partnering activities, particularly for ibezapolstat. Together with the grant opportunities, which are promising, and partnership discussions, some of which are well underway, the company may look at a smaller registered direct offering, warrant inducements, and other methods. It is likely that a combination of the above opportunities funds the company moving forward, and the expected timeline to hear back about grant approvals is in Q1.

Finances and Valuation

Acurx had $6.4 million in cash according to its latest quarterly filing (June 2024), and its TTM cash burn was $12.2 million. As such the company has less than 6 months of cash runway left unless it has been using its ATM facility since that quarterly report. Regardless, the company needs $25+ million or $50+ million to get it through its phase 3 trial(s), and this is a big hurdle if the company cannot get pharma or government interested. I previously calculated a share value of $9.81 per share for the C. diff program. I used a conservative risk-adjusted multiples approach. Here I will update my numbers. Using peak sales of $600 million (conservative, instead of $1 billion) in 2035 (approval estimated in 2026), a 3x peak sales multiple (lower than other biotech multiples of perhaps 4-5x due to the antibiotics market), a 60% chance of phase 3 success, and a fairly normal clinical stage discount rate of 15% adjusted to the present, I arrive at a valuation of $232 million. Using the updated shares outstanding of 16.25 million, I arrive at $14.29/share. I believe this is roughly in line with analysts’ targets for the stock. ($10 and $12).

Risks

The risks for this investment are virtually the same as from my prior article, where I stated:

The antibiotics market is a risky area of the already risky biotech sector. Microcap companies that are not turning profits and therefore require money to make progress are particularly risky in this difficult market environment and particularly poor market for biotechnology companies. Companies can go bankrupt or investors can be severely diluted during further fundraising rounds, particularly if done at poor valuations which further amplifies the ballooning of outstanding and fully diluted share counts.

Acurx’s phase 2 trial had an odd readout with vancomycin having unprecedented success in curing C. diff. While this is likely a fluke, it could potentially have something to do with enrollment criteria. Chances are good that ibezapolstat does indeed work much better than vancomycin due to other measured effects like sustained clinical response and changes to the gut microbiome. However, it will require a phase 3 to unequivocally prove this and this very slight doubt will remain until put to rest.

Lastly, financing the company is a primary risk as the company needs more money than the current total value of its market capitalization to advance through phase 3.

Conclusion

ACXP will probably reach new highs if the programs are funded. I expect $6-8 dollars if the company can land some grant money for its C. diff program. However, if they fail to lock in some non-dilutive funding, pain could be ahead for existing investors. All in all, the company’s antibiotics are sorely needed, and it would be a shame to see the company struggle to move forward based on a lack of interest from the government or big pharma entities. Given the uniqueness of the company’s programs and the excellent data generated so far, I believe the company will succeed in finding a source of funding aside from investor dilution.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Tesla: Elon Musk Disappoints Again
data.description:
Tesla, Inc.’s (NASDAQ:TSLA) stock has dropped after the robotaxi event that took place earlier this month, as the market questioned the ability of the company’s upcoming service to meaningfully impact the business’s financials anytime soon. Despite all the innovative products that Tesla has been revealing recently, the company itself remains an automaker that has been reporting relatively mixed earnings results this year. It has seen its stock depreciate YTD due to the ongoing price war in the electric vehicle sector.

Back in 2021, we already said that the issues that Tesla faces in China are only getting started, and more challenges are ahead. Since that time, the S&P 500 Index (SP500) saw a return of 34%, while Tesla’s stock trades at the same price as it was in 2021 when our last article on the business came out here on Seeking Alpha. Currently, we don’t see any major upside for Tesla and believe that its stock remains a SELL ahead of its Q3 release on October 23rd.

The Upside Is Not There Anymore

There was a lot of skepticism before the beginning of the Robotaxi event that took place earlier this month. That skepticism was more than justified. For a long time, the predictions that Tesla’s CEO Elon Musk was making failed to materialize plenty of times. Elon Musk’s prediction in 2016 that Tesla was less than two years away from complete autonomy didn’t play out. To this day, the company only has Level 2 autonomy. His prediction in 2019 that Tesla will have 1 million robotaxis in 2020 also failed to materialize.

The event that took place this month certainly was interesting, but also disappointed plenty of investors. Major outlets such as Bloomberg and others found out after the event that the Optimus robots were controlled by humans behind the scenes. At the same time, it’s also difficult to imagine that the autonomous FSD will be available in Texas and California next year, given that previous predictions about autonomy did not materialize and Tesla is still on Level 2 autonomy.

It's also questionable whether it will be possible for Tesla to launch Cybercabs, which were revealed during the event, before 2027 for less than $30,000. We should not forget that while Cybertruck was revealed in 2019, it entered volume production only last year, and it’s expected to be profitable only in 2025. Tesla Semi has a similar story. After the concept for the truck was revealed in 2017, it entered production only in 2022, while the volume production is expected only in 2026. That’s why there’s a risk that the production, and especially the volume production, of Cybercabs is manageable in less than three years from now.

What we can be certain about is that none of the products that were revealed earlier this month will have a meaningful impact on Tesla’s financials anytime soon. In a way, that’s an issue since the company is suffering from the rising competition that is slowly its market share.

Earlier this year, Tesla already executed another round of price cuts across the globe to attract new customers. This has already negatively affected Tesla’s bottom-line performance. The company’s gross profit margins are now below the sector’s median, while the performance in Q1 and Q2 was relatively weak. The company’s stock is also down 11% YTD, while the S&P 500 Index is up over 20% YTD.

The problem is that despite the price cuts, the competition continues to heat up. In Q3, Tesla delivered 462,890 vehicles, which was below the expectations of 463,310 vehicles. A few months ago, CEO Elon Musk himself said that the Chinese EV makers could demolish the competition, while the Chinese EV automaker BYD Company (OTCPK:BYDDF) is now expected to sell more vehicles than Tesla this year.

All of this points to the fact that the upside for Tesla is limited for now. The company is expected to grow its sales barely this year, while earnings are expected to be down Y/Y. That’s why we don’t expect many positives from the upcoming Q3 earnings report that should be out this Wednesday.

The Real Value of Tesla

With the lack of any meaningful upside this year, we believe that Tesla’s stock is an unattractive investment. Below, we present our valuation model, which will show why buying the company’s shares at the current price makes no sense.

Our valuation model covers the period of the next five years. The effective tax rate in the model is 21%, which is the current corporate standard rate in the United States. The perpetual growth rate is 3%, which is close to the historical GDP growth rate. We made this model when Tesla was trading at $220.89 per share, and the data for the cash reserves and the long-term debt is taken from the latest earnings report for Q2.

The discount rate in the model is 9.65%. We calculated this rate first by figuring out Tesla’s after-tax cost of debt and cost of equity rates. The numbers for the cost of debt calculations were taken from the recent earnings report. To find out the cost of equity we used the risk-free rate of 4.10%, beta of 1.59, and the market return rate of 7.69%. Thereafter, we weighted Tesla’s debt along with equity and found out the discount rate.

Once we got the initial data needed for our calculations, we then moved on to our forecast. In our model, we assume that Tesla will grow at a single-digit rate this year, which is also what the consensus currently is. In 2025 and beyond, we forecast a sales growth of 20%. This is above the current consensus by a few percentages, but there’s a possibility that analysts will do upward revisions in the future considering that the EV market is expanding, and Tesla’s top line is constantly improving.

The EBIT in 2024 is expected to be down Y/Y. The consensus is similar and a decline in earnings is expected, after which the EBIT is gradually forecasted to improve. We have reflected this in our model as well.

The D&A as a percentage of sales in the model is similar to the historical performance, and the change in net working capital remains positive as it was last year. The CapEx in 2024 is expected to be over $10 billion, which is what the management recently guided.

Once we completed our forecast table, we then moved on to calculating Tesla’s intrinsic value, which is presented on the right side of the screen below. After figuring out Tesla’s enterprise value, we add cash and subtract long-term debt to arrive at the equity value of $307.9 billion. Then we divide the equity value by the number of outstanding shares and find out that Tesla’s intrinsic value is $88.46 per share. This means that the downside is ~60% from the current market price.

While our model shows a significant downside to Tesla’s shares, there are nevertheless price targets that are lower than ours among major Wall Street firms. Even if we decrease the assumptions for the tax rate in the model to ~10%, then the intrinsic value would be around $100 per share, which also represents a significant downside. Since there’s a possibility that corporate taxes in the United States will increase in the coming years to combat the rising budget deficits, we believe that at the very least assuming a 21% rate in the model for the following years makes sense.

Furthermore, by trading close to 100 times its future earnings and significantly above the sector median, it’s difficult to see how Tesla could be considered a safe and attractive investment at the current price.

Risk to Our Bearish Thesis

One of the biggest risks to our bearish thesis is the fact that Tesla has been trading at an exuberant valuation for a long time, and that did not stop its shares from appreciating even further. That’s why the shares could perform well if there are enough positive developments that will keep investors excited about the company’s future.

While the price war within the electric vehicle sector is certainly a big deal, especially for the bottom-line performance, it benefits the top-line performance as cheaper vehicles become more attractive to consumers. We already see an increase in global EV sales, which is certainly a positive development for Tesla and its peers.

There’s also a possibility that 2025 will be a much better year for Tesla since it’s expected to launch a new affordable vehicle, which could drive its sales much higher. That’s the main reason why we slightly increased the sales growth rate in our model above the current expectations in 2025 and beyond. The startup production of the new vehicle is expected in early 2025, and it could certainly have a positive effect on Tesla’s shares. However, having only one major growth catalyst in the foreseeable future is not enough to support the growth story in our opinion.

Final Thoughts

Back in 2021, we saw that the EV environment is changing, and it’s going to be much tougher for Tesla to compete. We turned out to be correct, as Tesla’s stock has greatly underperformed against the S&P 500. Since that time, nothing much has changed, as the company continues to face the same challenges. While it’s able to scale its business and grow sales, the upside for its shares is simply not there, especially at the current market price. That’s why Tesla remains a SELL for us.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: How Truist's Solid Q3 Is (Slowly) Winning Back My Trust
data.description:
Introduction

Major regional bank Truist Financial Corp. (NYSE:TFC) released its third quarter 2024 results last Thursday. The report was insofar exciting because it concluded the first quarter of operations following the completion of the Truist Insurance Holdings divestiture in early May.

In this update, I share my thoughts on Truist's latest results. Recall that my last article was published before the second quarter results were released, but after U.S. Bancorp's (USB) strong half-year report, which once again underscored the superior quality of the larger competitor. It would be an exaggeration to say that I have lost confidence in Truist management, but the previous quarterly reports were quite disappointing, so some skepticism was (and probably still is) warranted. With that in mind, however, it's fair to say that with the Q3 report, my faith in Truist is returning, slowly but surely - let's see why...

Truist's Q3 Results Make Me Once Again More Confident With My Investment

Truist delivered adjusted earnings per share (EPS) of $0.97 for Q3 2024, about 8% above consensus. Total revenue - the taxable equivalent of net interest income and non-interest income - was $5.14 billion, down 10% from the prior year due to the sale of the insurance business and in line with consensus (an insignificant beat by $30 million).

Net interest income and non-interest income rose by 2.2% and 3.1%, respectively. The latter improved thanks to higher income from investment banking and trading income, the former due to a modest increase in the yield on loans to 6.41% (plus 3 basis points) and a more significant increase in the yield of the securities portfolio.

Truist's securities portfolio had an average yield of 2.97% in the third quarter, compared to 2.77% in the second quarter and 2.46% in the first quarter. So I would argue that most of the increase in yield is due to the selective investment of the proceeds from the sale of the remaining 80% stake in Truist Insurance Holdings into higher yielding securities. Management pointed out that the new investments have a relatively short duration. So if interest rates continue to fall, I expect a headwind for net interest margin from this perspective in the longer term. For more information on the repositioning of Truist's balance sheet following the sale of the insurance business, see for example slide 18 of the Q3 Fixed Income Presentation.

The improvement in loan and securities yields resulted in the net interest margin increasing for the second consecutive quarter, to 3.12%:

In addition to the solid improvement in net interest margin, it was also pleasing to see Truist's adjusted efficiency ratio improve to 55.2%, about 80 basis points better versus the prior quarter and a really strong 660 basis points better versus the third quarter of 2023. It was particularly reassuring to see the GAAP efficiency ratio broadly in line with the adjusted ratio, as shown in Figure 2. Of course, investors are probably no longer expecting the merger synergies announced in late 2019 to fully materialize, but I would argue that it's hard to deny that things are finally improving - slowly but surely.

Less positive was the update on Truist's loan portfolio - not in terms of credit quality, which remains solid (see below) - but in terms of volume. Average total loans totaled $303 billion in the quarter, a 1% decline from the previous quarter and a significant lag behind peer U.S. Bancorp (Figure 3). However, the particularly pronounced gap in the first quarter of 2023 shouldn't be overstated, due to U.S. Bancorp's acquisition of MUFG Union Bank from Mitsubishi UFJ Financial Group (MUFG) in late 2022.

The decline in Truist's average loan balance in the third quarter was largely due to moderate weakness in demand for commercial and residential mortgage loans, and management expects this pressure to continue through at least the end of the year. Weakness in residential mortgages was somewhat offset by higher demand for indirect auto and other consumer loans. This could be interpreted as a weak sign of improved demand in the face of (perceived) lower interest rates, but also as a sign of consumer weakness in the face of inflation-driven lower disposable incomes. Given the domestic credit quality ratios, I tend to lean towards the latter. While charge-offs on business loans are still at or only slightly above pre-pandemic levels, charge-offs on consumer loans have reached a level of almost 3%, more than 50 to 100 basis points higher than in the years 2012 to 2019.

Like the loan base, Truist's deposit base also declined slightly in the third quarter. Average total deposits for the quarter totaled $384 billion, down 1.0% from the previous quarter and in line with close peer U.S. Bancorp (Figure 4). Even though the same caveat as above applies to USB's first quarter 2023 deposits, I would argue that Truist's performance in recent years has been quite disappointing. According to Bill Rogers, Truist's CEO, digital account growth was quite strong with nearly 200,000 new accounts added in the quarter, 35% more than the third quarter of 2023. With interest rates falling, I think churn among banks should slow somewhat. However, I suspect that the continued decline in deposits could be a sign of a potentially underwhelming value proposition, so it's worth watching how deposits compare to the likes of U.S. Bancorp going forward.

As far as borrowing costs are concerned, I believe Truist (like other banks) has reached - or is close to reaching - a turning point. As shown in Figure 5, short-term borrowing costs are down for the second quarter in a row, and deposit costs were virtually unchanged from the second quarter. Long-term borrowing costs increased by 26 basis points, the largest increase in more than a year. According to Truist management, additional hedging costs were primarily responsible for this increase.

However, skeptical investors might interpret the increase in the long-term refinancing rate - against the backdrop of a lower benchmark rate - as a feeble sign of growing reservations among Truist's bondholders. That said, I have not found any arguments to support this hypothesis. Moody's Baa1 long-term credit rating was only affirmed with a stable outlook in mid-September, and I think it's also worth noting that the other two major rating agencies, S&P and Fitch Ratings, have even rated Truist's senior unsecured debt slightly higher, at A-. Also, a look at the spread of Truist's longer-duration bonds, such as the 2022/33 Senior Unsecured Notes, to the SOFR rate or to long-term Treasuries (e.g. US10Y) gave no cause for concern.

Finally, as already indicated, Truist's credit quality remains solid. Charge-offs, which exceeded management's expectations in Q3, declined for the second consecutive quarter and were 7 basis points below the domestic average in Q2 (Figure 6). I expect Q3 to yield a similarly positive comparison (the number has not yet been published on the Federal Reserve's website). Non-performing loans (NPLs) were slightly higher than in Q2, at 0.48% of all loans and leases. Interestingly, NPLs related to commercial real estate declined in the third quarter. The impact of Hurricane Helene on Truist's loan base appears to be very manageable, considering the recently established loan loss provision of just $25 million.

In terms of loss-absorbing capacity, it should be emphasized that Truist's Common Equity Tier 1 (CET1) ratio improved significantly as a result of the sale of the remaining 80% stake in Truist Insurance Holdings, from 10.1% at the end of 2023 to 11.6% at the end of the second and third quarters. However, this doesn't mean that Truist's CET1 ratio posed a significant risk before the transaction. Based on the CET1 ratio at the end of 2023, the bank performed adequately in the Federal Reserve's stress test for 2024. In the severely adverse stress scenario, Truist's CET1 ratio fell by 2.2 percentage points and was therefore well below the median value of 2.6 percentage points. Those interested in how the Fed's stress test is conducted, and in particular what assumptions are made, should have a look at my in-depth explanation published in a separate article.

Against this backdrop, it was not too surprising that Truist's Board of Directors approved a share buyback program of $5 billion at the end of June. So far, $500 million has been repurchased and management expects to buy back a further $500 million in the fourth quarter. If we assume that the buyback program is completed at an average price representing Truist's current share price of $43.5, approximately 8.5% of shares outstanding could be retired, excluding, of course, potential dilution from stock-based compensation. Given the solid capital levels and the greater emphasis on cash distributions to shareholders, I would not rule out a return to a growing dividend next year, provided of course that the results of the 2025 stress test and the economic environment allow this.

Conclusion

Truist reported its results for the third quarter of 2024 - the first full quarter excluding the insurance business - last Thursday. The market reacted somewhat negatively to what I consider to be a solid earnings report.

Granted, deposit and loan performance are still weak - even by comparison with some of Truist's peers, but part of the weakness is certainly due to generally subdued loan demand, so not necessarily a company-specific issue. The solid increase in the net interest margin, the improved efficiency ratio and the growing customer relationships were the highlights of the report. The return on total capital employed (ROTCE) was 13.8% at the end of Q3, and Truist is targeting a figure in the mid-teens going forward. In the longer term, a ROTCE more in line with what was promised at the end of 2019 could be achieved, but I would say that is a long way off.

I was also pleasantly surprised by the low charge-off ratio, which declined for the second quarter in a row and should once again be well below the national average. The significantly strengthened CET1 ratio, together with the balanced and good quality loan portfolio, should enable Truist to successfully navigate even a deep recession environment. Of course, one could argue that Truist is less diversified after the sale of the remaining 80% stake in Truist Insurance Holdings, but this is an aspect that I would not overemphasize. After all, the insurance business only contributed about 14% of Truist's total revenues in 2023, and the bank received more than $10 billion in after-tax proceeds.

In my view, the recent approval of a $5 billion share buyback program confirms that the sale of the insurance business was not a desperate move to bolster the bank's capitalization. While I expect management to retire the shares only gradually, the resulting lower dividend payout ratio combined with the improved (and hopefully stabilizing) efficiency ratio could provide a solid foundation for a return to sustainable dividend growth.

All in all, I have a more constructive view on Truist than in my last article. In July, I contemplated shifting my TFC position into shares of other banks, such as U.S. Bancorp. While I have increased my USB position in the third quarter, I have not touched my position in TFC, and unless the situation worsens as a result of managerial missteps, I feel comfortable holding on to my position.

Thank you very much for reading my latest article. Whether you agree or disagree with my conclusions, I always welcome your opinion and feedback in the comments below. And if there's anything I should improve or expand on in future articles, drop me a line as well. As always, please consider this article only as a first step in your own due diligence.

data.title: Sony Should Appreciate And Potentially Break Out
data.description:
Sony Group Corporation (NYSE:SONY) is an undervalued conglomerate that is likely to reprice higher in the coming quarters on the strength of its intellectual property and financial services, among other segments that may perform well. Sony has multiple segments that have significant potential to grow revenue, including gaming, pictures, and music. Sony may also benefit from a continued revaluation within the semiconductor industry, where Sony's CMOS products are likely to remain competitive and have the potential to grow their margin of profitability.

Sony’s most apparent growth prospect is the continued effective monetization of its entertainment related intellectual property, including its movies, television programming, and gaming. One of Sony's current strengths is that, early on, it realized that it was not profitable for it to develop its own streaming service that would compete with Netflix, YouTube, and Prime Video. Rather than develop a costly competing service, like its competing production houses have, that might take years, if ever, to gain the critical mass of subscribers to achieve profitability, Sony is licensing its intellectual property to its peers and prioritize content profitability over exclusivity.

Instead of making a broad streaming service, Sony opted to develop a service specializing in anime and gaming. Sony acquired Crunchyroll in 2021 and merged it with Funimation, the Japanese anime distributor Sony acquired in 2017. Crunchyroll is now run as an independently operated joint venture between the U.S.-based Sony Pictures Entertainment and Japan-based Aniplex, which is owned by Sony Music Entertainment. Through Aniplex, Sony produces content such as Inu-Oh, Demon Slayer and Solo Leveling that it makes available on Crunchyroll and other services.

While Crunchyroll is a distribution system for Sony's produced anime content, a majority of the content that the service distributes is made by other Japanese anime creators. Crunchyroll has shared audience insight and engagement preferences data with those production partners in an effort to maintain its marketplace dominance. While Sony does not give regular updates on the specific revenue it derives from, Crunchyroll, which it includes in its pictures segment, Sony recently noted that "Cruncyroll has more than 130 million users in more than 200 countries and regions around the world."

Sony also called out Crunchyroll when it last reported earnings, commenting that it realized "higher revenues for Crunchyroll mainly due to paid subscriber growth" while also noting decreases to Sony's series deliveries in television productions and theatrical releases. Given that it noted decreases in movies and TV, but increases in Cruncyroll, this implies that the five percent in increase to Sony Pictures' total sales was largely due to the strength by Crunchyroll.

The market does not fully appreciate the value of Crunchyroll's paid subscriber base, as well as its likelihood to continue to grow in the coming years. Moreover, it is likely that Sony will continue to find ways to further enhance revenue through the service, including gaming and merchandising opportunities that would be marketable to its subscribers.

Sony could eventually spin off Crunchyroll, or simply provide greater insight into its performance metrics, but in either case it should appreciate in value and benefit Sony shares. Moreover, the paid subscription model that Sony is currently growing within its pictures segment is more stable than the segment's traditional business model, which should afford it a higher multiple over time.

Sony's Game & Network Services Segment is its largest revenue producer, and continues to benefit from the strong performance of its PlayStation products. This includes an ongoing migration of systems from the PS4 to the PS5. PlayStation is likely to benefit from growth in virtual reality, and is also likely to be a source of strength to the Crunchyroll service, as well as subscriptions to PlayStation Plus. It appears likely that PlayStation Plus now has enough subscribers to ensure profitability and that any continued growth will be a significant driver of improved operating margins for the service and the segment in total.

Sony's music segment also continues to perform well, with growing revenue from merchandising and live events from its recorded music division, as well as increased revenue from streaming services out of both recorded music and music publishing divisions. This segment recognized a significant benefit from the weaker Japanese Yen, where the vast majority of the revenue it derived was from outside of Japan.

In the nearer term, Sony is likely to benefit from the already planned partial spin off and listing of the Sony Financial Group in October of 2025. Under the plan, Sony will spin off approximately 80 percent of Sony Financial Group. Last quarter, Sony Financial Group's revenue came in at 232.8 billion yen, which was a 34 percent decrease versus a year earlier. This lumpy revenue has been a problematic contributor to the conglomerate. Spinning off the Sony Financial Group should reduce Sony's overall earnings volatility, and potentially allow the financial unit to grow more aggressively on its own.

Moreover, it appears that financial companies are currently undergoing an increased valuation. This should benefit Sony and its pending spin off of Sony Financial Group, where the segment is likely to have increased in valuation since announcing the plan last year. Financial equities have been strong performers over the last few weeks, with the potential for them to undergo multiple expansion as interest rates are cut. This should be a tailwind for Sony Financial Group and provide it with the potential for a strong market valuation when it is spun off next year.

Sony shares have been range bound for much of 2024 and appear to be in the middle of that range at the moment. Sony may find some level of supply at about $18 per share. If this level should fail, it appears likely that Sony shares would find significant support at about $17, which coincides with the bottom of the ascending trading range in which shares now sit.

A breach of that level could put its spring and summer lows back in play, which were at about $15 and $16, respectively. I believe these levels are unlikely, but certainly not impossible. Similarly, it is not outside the realm of possibility for shares to appreciate to the top of their trading range on a strong earnings report in mid November, which would be between $19.50 and $20 per share.

If shares could break above $20, it would indicate shares have a high likelihood of further appreciation on multiple expansion. This could be due to anticipation of the Sony Financial Group spin off, a recognition of the strength of Crunchyroll's growing subscription service revenue, or the growth from the PlayStation Plus service. In any case, a break out of apparent resistance at around $20 could indicate shares are ready to break out and back towards the highs Sony reached in late 2021, at around $26.

Another source of strength for Sony could be from its image sensor business, which could benefit from a strong refresh cycle within the smartphone market. Sony is a market leader in image sensors, where their high quality product helps improve image quality. As a result, Sony has been the primary image sensor suppler to Apple's iPhone. While Sony does not realize a significant margin of profitability from this business, a strong smartphone refresh cycle is likely to contribute to a move higher.

Risks to Sony would include a weak smartphone refresh and/or upgrade cycle that results in poor operating margins out of image sensors. Without a strong smartphone refresh cycle, Sony's image sensors may actually fail to reach profitability in coming quarters. Another risk is that the high price of PlayStation 5 and PlayStation VR 2 units will result in low continued sales. Moreover, smartphones could provide a reasonable alternative in the form of augmented reality capabilities.

Other risks include weakness out of Sony's upcoming films, as well as a poor performance out of the spin-off of Sony Financial Group.

Conclusion

Sony is trading below its fair value and represents a strong value at risk. Sony could also benefit from continued revaluation of the Japanese stock market, with the Nikkei now trading at levels not seen in decades. Sony has been developing strong subscription service revenue out of Crunchyroll and PlayStation Plus, which should warrant an increased valuation. If shares were to decline from here, they are likely to find strong support at around $17. If the current smartphone refresh cycle is strong, and the spin-off of Sony Financial Group goes well, it is likely that Sony shares will soon break out of their multi-year trading range and potentially test their 2021 high at around $26 per share.
